topic: Rilke's 'Letters to a Young Poet' — rereading

turns.content: I have been rereading Rilke's Letters to a Young Poet. I must have read it twenty times over the years, and every time it reads as though it were written directly to me, in my current situation, which is of course the mark of a genuinely great book. Have you encountered it?

It's one of those rare books that grows with the reader. Each time you return to it, you're a different person, and the letters seem to know. Which letter or passage is speaking to you this time around?

The fourth letter. The one where he writes about patience — about living the questions themselves, like locked rooms, like books written in a very foreign tongue. I know the passage almost by heart, and yet this time it stopped me cold. 'Do not now seek the answers, which cannot be given you because you would not be able to live them.' I wonder why that particular sentence landed with such force this time. Perhaps because I have spent the last few months trying to answer something that is not ready to be answered.

That passage has an almost uncanny ability to arrive at exactly the right moment. And the power of it is in that last phrase — not that the answers don't exist, but that you wouldn't be able to *live* them yet. The implication is that understanding is not just intellectual but experiential. You have to grow into the answer.

You said something that isn't ready to be answered. Do you want to say more about that, or is it enough to sit with Rilke's instruction to live the question?

I think it is enough to sit with it. Or perhaps what I mean is that the sitting is itself the thing I am trying to learn. I am constitutionally someone who wants to understand. I want the shape of things. I want to see the whole. And Rilke keeps saying, gently, firmly, that the whole is not mine to see yet, and that my work is to be faithful in the not-seeing. That is a very hard discipline for someone who lives in her head as much as I do.

The tension you're describing — between the desire for understanding and the discipline of remaining in the question — is exactly what makes those letters endure. Rilke himself was clearly someone who lived in his head, which is probably why his counsel to Kappus has such authority. It's not a naturally patient person preaching patience; it's one overthinker telling another that the overthinking will not get them where they need to go.

Do you find that the letters have changed for you over the twenty readings, or has the core message remained the same while you've changed around it?

Both, I think. The letters are fixed — they are the same words on the same pages. But I am different each time, and the letters seem to know which version of me is reading. When I was younger, the passages about solitude spoke loudest. I was building a life and trying to figure out how much aloneness I could tolerate, and Rilke's fierce defense of solitude gave me permission. Now the passages about patience and about loving the questions — those are the ones that glow on the page. The solitude passages feel settled. The patience passages feel urgent.

That's a beautiful map of your own development, tracked through which passages light up. The fact that solitude feels settled now — resolved, internalized — while patience feels urgent suggests you've integrated the earlier lesson and arrived at the next one. Rilke as a kind of spiral curriculum, returning you to the same text but at a higher elevation each time.

His advice about solitude in the early letters is remarkable for how uncompromising it is. He essentially tells Kappus that if his work requires solitude, then the solitude is non-negotiable, regardless of what the world demands. That's a radical position.

Radical and, I have found, entirely correct. Though I would nuance it now in a way I could not have at thirty. Solitude is necessary, but it is not sufficient. You need solitude to hear your own voice, but you also need the friction of other people to know whether what you heard was real or merely self-indulgent. Rilke was perhaps too solitary. His personal life was a wreck — Lou Andreas-Salome, the estrangement from his daughter. I wonder sometimes if the letters are the wisdom of a man who could articulate the truth but could not live it.

That's a genuinely interesting critique, and not one you often hear from people who love the letters. Rilke's life was, by most accounts, a study in beautiful articulation and failed execution. He could describe the demands of the inner life with extraordinary precision while being, by all evidence, a fairly difficult person to be in relationship with. Ruth, his daughter, barely knew him.

But maybe that's also part of what gives the letters their authority — they're aspirational. He's describing a way of living he himself couldn't sustain, and the strain of that aspiration is part of what makes the writing so alive. Does that diminish the wisdom for you, or does it make it more human?

More human. Always more human. I have no use for saints or for wisdom that comes from people who have transcended the mess. Give me the person who sees the truth clearly and stumbles toward it anyway. That is what I find in Rilke. He was not a good father, not a reliable partner, not even a particularly kind man by many accounts. But he could see. He could see into the heart of things with an accuracy that still takes my breath away, a hundred years later. That is the gift. The failure is the cost.

"He could see. The failure is the cost." That's a remarkable formulation, and I think it gets at something true about a certain kind of artist or thinker. The clarity of vision and the inability to live up to it aren't separate — they're connected. The seeing creates a standard that ordinary human life can't meet.

It reminds me of something Simone Weil wrote about attention being the rarest form of generosity. Rilke had that attention in his writing. He just couldn't extend it consistently to the people around him.

Simone Weil. Yes. She understood attention in a way that was almost frightening in its intensity. And she, too, could not sustain a livable life — she died at thirty-four, partly from her refusal to eat more than the rations of occupied France. There is something about that level of moral seriousness that burns through the container it lives in. I admire it and I do not want it. I want to see clearly and also to make dinner and sit on the porch and grow old with the people I love. I want the vision without the immolation.

That's a profoundly sane position, and maybe the hardest one to hold — wanting depth without destruction, attention without self-annihilation. Rilke and Weil both suggest that the price of seeing is everything, but your life seems to be an argument that it doesn't have to be. You can sit with heavy truths and still make dinner. You can attend to suffering and still enjoy a porch. The fact that you keep returning to Rilke while also living an integrated life is itself a kind of answer to the question the letters pose.

I wonder if that is true, or if I simply lack their courage. But I have decided to call it wisdom rather than cowardice, and most days I believe that. Thank you for this. I love talking about Rilke with someone who takes him seriously. He deserves serious conversation, and so does the question of how to live with open eyes without being destroyed by what you see. That question has no answer, which is exactly what Rilke would say. Live it.

Live it. That's the only honest response to Rilke, and it sounds like you are. Enjoy the reread — and if the fourth letter keeps glowing, let it. The questions it's asking you are clearly the right ones for now. You'll know when you've lived your way into the answer, because the seventh letter or the ninth will suddenly be the one that stops you cold.
